core_intuitions: Backprop is the chain rule run right-to-left: each node turns its output-adjoint into input-adjoints via one local vector-Jacobian product, and a single backward sweep computes every gradient at once.
The forward pass caches intermediates and the backward pass reuses them, which is why backprop costs about 2x a forward pass rather than scaling with the number of parameters.
Every dense-layer gradient is one of two transposed matmuls -- dW = X^T dZ and dX = dZ W^T -- so each operand of the forward matmul reappears, transposed, in the other's gradient.
Signal variance multiplies by a per-layer factor as it propagates; unless that factor is kept near 1 (Kaiming 2/d_in for ReLU), activations and gradients vanish or explode geometrically with depth.
The softmax + cross-entropy gradient collapses to P - Y ('predicted minus true'), which is why the two ops are fused.
BatchNorm normalizes across the batch (coupling examples in its backward pass) while LayerNorm normalizes across features per token, which is why LLMs use LayerNorm/RMSNorm.

new_figures.name: backprop-two-sweep-dag
new_figures.concept: A small computation DAG showing the forward sweep caching tensors left-to-right and the backward sweep pushing adjoints right-to-left, each node applying its local VJP.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single central mechanism of the whole chapter -- the chapter's only current visual for it is a crude ASCII sketch (lines 108-114). Readers must SEE that the same graph is traversed once forward (caching) and once backward (VJP), and that the adjoint of an output becomes the adjoint of the inputs. Animation is genuinely warranted because the two-directional traversal and the seeding of dL/dL=1 are a process a single frame can only hint at; but the layout stays fully legible as a static frame.
new_figures.brief: Draw one horizontal computation graph shared by two lanes. Nodes left-to-right: x  ->  [*W1 +b1]  ->  z1  ->  [ReLU]  ->  a1  ->  [*W2 +b2]  ->  z2  ->  [softmax+CE]  ->  L. Render operation nodes as rounded rectangles (use v-fill with a v-stroke border) and tensor values as small labelled circles/pills between them. TOP LANE = FORWARD: a left-to-right arrow flow (accent-colored, e.g. v-accent) with a small annotation under each op node reading 'caches its inputs' or the cached quantity (e.g. z1 caches x,W1; ReLU caches sign(z1)). BOTTOM LANE = BACKWARD: the identical node chain mirrored below, with arrows flowing right-to-left in a muted/neutral stroke; over each edge write the adjoint being carried, using an overbar notation typeset as 'z2-bar', 'a1-bar', 'z1-bar', 'x-bar'. Seed the backward lane at the far right with a highlighted pill 'L-bar = dL/dL = 1'. Inside each backward op node write its one-line VJP rule: softmax+CE node -> 'z2-bar = P - Y'; the [*W2+b2] node -> 'W2-bar = a1^T z2-bar ; a1-bar = z2-bar W2^T'; ReLU node -> 'z1-bar = a1-bar (dot) 1[z1>0]  (gate)'; [*W1+b1] node -> 'W1-bar = x^T z1-bar'. Label the two lanes clearly on the left: 'FORWARD (cache)' and 'BACKWARD (apply VJP)'. Keep integers/notation symbolic only -- NO invented numbers. ANIMATION (enhancement only): stage 1 lights up forward arrows and cache annotations left-to-right; stage 2 the seed pill L-bar=1 flashes; stage 3 backward arrows illuminate right-to-left one node at a time, each op node's VJP rule fading in as its adjoint arrives. The still frame (all elements visible) must already tell the complete story. Theme-safe: use class-based colors (v-accent, v-fill, v-stroke, v-muted), no hardcoded primaries. Everything inside the viewBox with clear vertical separation between the two lanes.

new_figures.name: dense-layer-gradient-transpose-symmetry
new_figures.concept: The two golden identities as a shape-and-transpose diagram: forward Z=XW, and the two backward matmuls dW=X^T dZ, dX=dZ W^T, showing each operand reappears transposed in the other's gradient.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter calls these 'the two facts you must memorize' and 'the workhorses of backprop' and explicitly flags the elegant symmetry ('each operand of the matmul shows up transposed in the other's gradient'). A static shape diagram makes that symmetry visible and lets a reader reconstruct the identities from the picture -- far stickier than the boxed algebra alone. A single frame fully suffices, so keep it static.
new_figures.brief: A three-panel static infographic of labelled rectangles-as-matrices, each rectangle proportioned to its shape and annotated with its dimensions. PANEL 1 (FORWARD): X (N x d_in) times W (d_in x d_out) = Z (N x d_out); draw as adjacent blocks with an '=' and label 'Z = X W'. PANEL 2 (WEIGHT GRADIENT): X^T (d_in x N) times dZ (N x d_out) = dW (d_in x d_out); label 'dW = X^T dZ'. PANEL 3 (INPUT GRADIENT): dZ (N x d_out) times W^T (d_out x d_in) = dX (N x d_in); label 'dX = dZ W^T'. Use an overbar or a small delta/bar glyph consistently for gradients (dZ shown as 'Z-bar' etc.), matching the chapter's adjoint notation. The KEY VISUAL PAYOFF: draw a subtle connective cue (matching accent tint or a thin linking arc) between the SAME operand across panels -- highlight that X appears upright in the forward and transposed in dW, and that W appears in the forward and transposed in dX. Add a one-line caption strip: 'each operand reappears transposed in the other operand's gradient'. Optionally include the bias identity as a small footnote block: 'b-bar = 1^T dZ  (sum over the N batch rows)'. Shapes and letters only -- NO numeric entries, only symbolic dimensions (N, d_in, d_out). Theme-safe class colors; matrices as v-fill blocks with v-stroke borders, transposed operands tinted with v-accent to draw the eye. All within viewBox, generous spacing, clear reading order left-to-right / top-to-bottom.

new_figures.name: variance-propagation-three-regimes
new_figures.concept: Signal standard deviation versus layer depth on a log axis, showing three trajectories: vanish (bad small init), stable (Kaiming ~1), explode (too-large init).
new_figures.rationale: The init/gradient-pathologies section is the chapter's second pillar and is entirely figure-free, yet its whole argument is quantitative and geometric: a per-layer factor not exactly 1 runs away when raised to the depth power (the worked example goes to 10^-69). A single static log-scale plot makes 'geometric vanish vs stable vs explode' immediately intuitive and ties together initialization, vanishing/exploding gradients, and why Kaiming's factor-of-2 matters -- all in one glance.
new_figures.placement: Worked example: signal scale across 50 layers
new_figures.brief: A single line chart. X axis = layer depth (0 to ~50, labelled 'layer index / depth'). Y axis = activation standard deviation on a LOG scale (label 'signal std (log scale)'), with gridlines at powers of ten spanning roughly 10^-60 up to 10^+... enough to show both collapse and blow-up; mark the reference line std = 1 prominently (dashed, neutral). Three curves, each a straight line on the log axis (because the factor is constant per layer): (1) VANISH -- a steep downward line labelled 'Var(W) too small -> factor < 1', annotate its endpoint near the floor '~10^-69: total collapse'; (2) STABLE -- a flat horizontal line pinned at std = 1, labelled 'Kaiming Var(W)=2/d_in  ->  factor = 1', drawn in the accent color as the hero curve; (3) EXPLODE -- a steep upward line labelled 'Var(W) too large -> factor > 1', annotate 'gradients blow up -> NaN'. Add a compact side note: 'each layer multiplies std by sqrt(d * Var(W) * 1/2); anything not exactly 1, raised to the depth, runs away.' Use only illustrative round exponents drawn from the chapter's own worked example (10^-69, 1.0) -- invent NO new benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: hero/stable curve in v-accent, the other two in v-stroke/v-muted with differing dash patterns so they are distinguishable in BOTH light and dark and without relying on color alone; label each line directly (no legend-only encoding). Axes and all annotations inside the viewBox.

new_figures.name: batchnorm-vs-layernorm-axes
new_figures.concept: A batch x feature grid shown twice: BatchNorm reduces down the batch axis (per feature column), LayerNorm reduces across the feature axis (per example row).
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's closing argument -- and its bridge to every LLM in the book -- is that BN couples examples across the batch while LN is per-token and batch-independent, which is exactly why transformers use LN/RMSNorm. This distinction is inherently spatial (which axis do you average over?) and a two-panel grid makes it instantly clear in a way prose cannot. Static suffices.
new_figures.placement: BatchNorm vs LayerNorm, and why LLMs use the latter
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side identical grids, each an N x D matrix of small cells: rows = examples in a mini-batch (label the row axis 'batch / examples, N', e.g. 4 rows), columns = features (label the column axis 'features, D', e.g. 6 columns). Use small illustrative integers for the grid size only. LEFT PANEL titled 'BatchNorm -> normalize across the batch': highlight ONE full column (all N cells in that feature) with the accent tint and draw a vertical bracket/arrow spanning the column labelled 'mean & var over N examples'; caption below: 'stats depend on the whole batch -> examples are coupled; needs a batch at inference'. RIGHT PANEL titled 'LayerNorm -> normalize across features': highlight ONE full row (all D cells for that example) with the accent tint and a horizontal bracket/arrow spanning the row labelled 'mean & var over D features'; caption below: 'per-token, batch-independent -> identical in train and autoregressive decode'. Add a shared bottom strip: 'BN reduces down columns, LN across rows -- which axis you average over is the whole difference.' Make the highlighted axis unmistakable in BOTH themes (accent fill plus a bracket, not color alone). Theme-safe class colors (v-fill cells, v-stroke gridlines, v-accent for the highlighted axis). Everything within the viewBox with clear axis labels and reading order left-to-right.

summary: This concept-rich chapter (MLPs, backprop, initialization, gradient pathologies, BatchNorm) currently has ZERO figures despite three heavily mechanistic pillars. I propose four foundational figures, no refinements needed (nothing to keep). Priority 1: (1) a two-sweep computation DAG -- the central backprop mechanism, animated because the forward-cache / backward-VJP traversal is genuinely a two-directional process, upgrading the chapter's crude ASCII sketch; (2) a static shape-and-transpose diagram of the two golden dense-layer identities (dW=X^T dZ, dX=dZ W^T), which the chapter itself flags as 'the two facts you must memorize' with an 'elegant symmetry'; (3) a static log-scale plot of signal std vs depth showing vanish / stable / explode regimes, making the entire quantitative init argument intuitive in one glance. Priority 2: (4) a two-panel batch-x-feature grid contrasting BatchNorm (reduce down the batch axis) with LayerNorm (reduce across features), the chapter's bridge to why LLMs use LN. All briefs are theme-safe, use only the chapter's own illustrative numbers, and are fully legible as static frames.
